name: Kalua Pork
steps: Roast the pork, covered, at 300F for 2.5-3 hours until it shreds easily.
Serve over the rice with the cabbage.

ingredients_full.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Enriched Medium Grain Calrose Rice

steps_detailed: Season the pork well and roast, covered tightly, at 300F (150C) for 2.5-3 hours, until it falls apart easily when pulled with two forks - kalua pork is traditionally cooked underground in an imu pit lined with hot stones and banana leaves for hours; a low, slow, tightly covered oven roast approximates that same long, moist heat.
Shred the pork and toss with a little of its own rendered juices.
Cook the calrose rice in 0.9 cup water, covered, 16-18 minutes.
Soften the cabbage briefly in a little of the pork drippings.
Serve the pulled pork over the rice with the cabbage alongside.

name: Huli Huli Chicken
steps: Marinate chicken in soy sauce and brown sugar 15 minutes.
Grill 6-7 minutes per side, basting occasionally.
steps_detailed: Whisk the soy sauce, brown sugar, ginger, garlic, and a splash of the reserved pineapple juice together for the marinade.
Coat the chicken and marinate at least 30 minutes, or overnight for deeper flavor.
Grill over medium-high heat, 6-7 minutes per side, basting with extra marinade partway through ('huli' means 'turn' in Hawaiian, referring to flipping the chicken repeatedly while basting).
Serve with the pineapple chunks alongside.

name: Loco Moco
steps: Cook the rice.
Pan-fry the beef patty and fry the egg.
Stack together.

ingredients_full.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Enriched Medium Grain Calrose Rice

steps_detailed: Cook the calrose rice in 1.1 cups water, covered, 18-20 minutes, until tender and slightly sticky.
Pan-fry the beef patty, 3-4 minutes per side, then set aside; soften the onion in the same pan.
Whisk the cornstarch into a little water and stir into the pan drippings and onion, simmering 2-3 minutes until it thickens into a simple brown gravy.
Fry the egg sunny-side up.
Stack the rice, patty, and egg, and pour the gravy over.
Loco moco's rice needs to be the sticky, clingy calrose style specifically - it's built to hold its shape under a ladle of gravy, which loose-grain jasmine or basmati wouldn't do nearly as well.

name: Ahi Poke Bowl
steps: Cook the rice.
Toss the fish with the soy sauce and serve over the rice - if aiming for the real dish, use sushi-grade raw ahi tuna, prepared according to safe raw-fish handling practices.

ingredients_full.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Enriched Medium Grain Calrose Rice

ingredients_full.note: standing in for raw ahi tuna, cubed
tsp, standing in for toasted sesame oil

steps_detailed: Cook the calrose rice in 1.1 cups water, covered, 18-20 minutes.

Note: authentic poke is made with raw, sushi-grade ahi tuna, which needs to be eaten raw and isn't something this recipe database can respons­ibly represent with a cook-first ingredient - tilapia here is listed as a placeholder shape for the dish's structure but should be cooked through if you're actually making this, not treated as sushi-grade.
If using raw, sushi-grade fish at home, toss the cubed fish with the soy sauce, oil, and onion, and chill 10 minutes before serving.
Serve over the rice.

name: Teriyaki Beef
steps: Marinate beef in soy sauce and brown sugar 15 minutes.
Sear 2-3 minutes per side.
steps_detailed: Whisk the soy sauce, brown sugar, garlic, and ginger together. Coat the beef and marinate at least 20 minutes.
Heat a splash of oil in a skillet over high heat and sear the beef, 2-3 minutes per side, until caramelized at the edges.
Top with sesame seeds before serving - teriyaki reflects Hawaii's deep Japanese immigrant influence, now a plate-lunch staple.

name: Pork and Greens (Lau Lau-Style)
steps: Brown the pork, 5-6 minutes.
Add spinach and soy sauce with 1/2 cup water, simmer 25-30 minutes until pork is tender.

ingredients_full.note: or collard greens, substitute for taro (luau) leaves

steps_detailed: Heat a splash of oil in a heavy pot over medium-high heat and brown the pork, 5-6 minutes.
Add the ginger, soy sauce, and broth. Bring to a simmer.
Cover and cook 25-30 minutes until the pork is very tender.

Add the spinach in batches, letting each wilt before adding more, and cook 5 more minutes - traditional lau lau wraps pork and butterfish in taro leaves and steams the whole bundle, but this stovetop version keeps the same comforting flavor.

name: Hawaiian BBQ Short Ribs
steps: Marinate beef in soy sauce and pineapple juice 20 minutes.
Grill 3-4 minutes per side.
steps_detailed: Blend or mash the pineapple chunks with their juice, then whisk with soy sauce, brown sugar, garlic, and sesame oil for the marinade.
Coat the beef and marinate at least 30 minutes, or overnight for deeper flavor - pineapple's enzymes help tenderize the meat, a classic Hawaiian technique.
Grill over medium-high heat, 3-4 minutes per side, until caramelized and cooked to preference.

name: Spam Musubi
steps: Cook the rice.
Press into a block shape.

ingredients_full.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Enriched Medium Grain Calrose Rice

steps_detailed: Cook the calrose rice in 1.1 cups water, covered, 18-20 minutes, until tender and sticky enough to hold a compact shape.

Simmer the soy sauce and sugar together 1-2 minutes until slightly syrupy, for glazing the canned meat traditionally used here (spam isn't a distinct database item, so this recipe centers on the rice and glaze structure).
Press the warm rice into a small rectangular mold or shape by hand into a block.
Musubi's calrose rice needs real stickiness to hold its shape once sliced - this is the same rice tradition that runs through loco moco and poke bowls across Hawaiian cooking generally.

name: Chicken Katsu Plate
steps: Cook the rice.
Dredge and fry the chicken, 4-5 minutes per side.
Serve over the rice.

ingredients_full.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Enriched Medium Grain Calrose Rice

steps_detailed: Cook the calrose rice in 1.1 cups water, covered, 18-20 minutes.
Dredge the chicken in the flour, then the beaten egg, coating well.
Fry in the oil over medium heat, 4-5 minutes per side, until deeply golden and cooked through.
Slice and serve over the rice with the soy sauce drizzled over.

Katsu's crisp, golden crust and the rice's stickiness underneath it are both central to a proper Hawaiian plate lunch - this is a lighter home version of what's traditionally coated in panko breadcrumbs for extra crunch, which isn't a distinct item here, so flour-and-egg carries the crust instead.

name: Poke Bowl (Lunch Size)
steps: Cook the rice.
Toss the fish with the soy sauce and serve over the rice.

ingredients_full.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Enriched Medium Grain Calrose Rice

ingredients_full.note: standing in for raw ahi tuna
tsp, standing in for toasted sesame oil

steps_detailed: Cook the calrose rice in 0.9 cup water, covered, 16-18 minutes.

As with the full-size poke bowl, real poke uses raw, sushi-grade tuna - if preparing this at home, use fish specifically labeled sushi-grade and follow safe raw-fish handling; the tilapia listed here should be cooked through otherwise.
Toss the fish with the soy sauce and oil, and serve over the rice.

name: Macaroni Salad with Chicken
steps: Toss cooled macaroni with chicken and mayonnaise.

ingredients_full.note: cooked until quite soft, then cooled

steps_detailed: Cook the macaroni a bit past al dente - authentic Hawaiian mac salad wants the pasta soft, not firm, so it absorbs the dressing well.
Cool completely, then toss with the chicken, carrot, mayonnaise, and milk.
Chill before serving - mac salad is the essential third component of any proper Hawaiian plate lunch.

name: Portuguese Sausage and Eggs
steps: Cook the rice.
Season the beef with the paprika, form patties, and pan-fry.
Scramble the eggs and serve together.

ingredients_full.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Enriched Medium Grain Calrose Rice

ingredients_full.note: standing in for Portuguese sausage, seasoned assertively

steps_detailed: Cook the calrose rice in 0.9 cup water, covered, 16-18 minutes.

Mix the beef with the paprika and garlic, form into small patties, and pan-fry, 3-4 minutes per side - Portuguese sausage (linguica) isn't a distinct item here, so heavily paprika-and-garlic-seasoned beef stands in for its smoky, garlicky character.
Scramble or fry the eggs.
Serve together with the rice, in the classic Hawaiian plate-lunch arrangement.

name: Hawaiian-Style French Toast
steps: Dip bread in beaten egg, pan-fry 2-3 minutes per side.
Top with pineapple.
steps_detailed: Whisk the egg and milk together. Dip the bread slices, coating both sides well.
Pan-fry over medium heat, 2-3 minutes per side, until golden.
Top with pineapple chunks, toasted coconut, and a drizzle of honey - island flavors on a familiar breakfast classic, a popular brunch item across the islands.

name: Spam Musubi Bites
steps: Cook the rice and press into small blocks.

ingredients_full.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Enriched Medium Grain Calrose Rice

steps_detailed: Cook the calrose rice in 0.75 cup water, covered, 16-18 minutes, until tender and sticky.
Simmer the soy sauce and sugar together briefly until slightly syrupy.
Press the warm rice into small bite-sized blocks.

name: Macadamia Nut Snack
steps: Enjoy as-is or lightly toasted in a dry skillet 2-3 minutes.
steps_detailed: Toss the macadamia nuts with honey.
Toast in a dry skillet over medium-low heat, 3-4 minutes, stirring often, until fragrant and lightly caramelized.
Cool before eating - macadamias are one of Hawaii's signature crops and a beloved local snack.

name: Dried Pineapple Snack
steps: Pat pineapple dry, bake at 250F for 1.5-2 hours until chewy and dried.
steps_detailed: Preheat the oven to 250F. Pat the pineapple very dry and slice thin if in large chunks.
Toss with a light dusting of cinnamon.
Spread on a lined baking sheet and bake 1.5-2 hours, flipping halfway, until chewy and dried.
Cool completely before eating - a naturally sweet, portable snack.

name: Haupia (Coconut Pudding)
steps: Whisk cornstarch into a little cold coconut milk, combine with remaining milk and honey.
Cook over medium heat, stirring, until thickened, about 6-8 minutes. Chill until set.
steps_detailed: Whisk the cornstarch into a splash of the cold coconut milk until smooth.
Combine with the remaining coconut milk, honey, and vanilla in a saucepan.
Cook over medium heat, whisking constantly, 6-8 minutes until thickened.
Pour into small cups and chill at least 2 hours until fully set - haupia is a traditional Hawaiian coconut pudding, a fixture at every luau.

name: Malasadas-Style Bites
steps: Make a simple sweet dough, shape into small balls, fry until golden, roll in sugar.
steps_detailed: Mix the flour, egg, sugar, milk, and melted butter into a soft, slightly sticky dough.
Shape into small balls.
Heat oil in a skillet over medium heat and fry 2-3 minutes per side until deeply golden and cooked through.
Roll the warm bites in sugar - malasadas arrived with Portuguese immigrants and are now a beloved Hawaiian treat, especially famous from Leonard's Bakery in Honolulu.

name: Grilled Mahi Mahi
steps: Marinate fish in soy sauce and ginger 10 minutes.
Grill or pan-sear 3-4 minutes per side.

ingredients_full.note: substitute for mahi mahi or other firm white fish

steps_detailed: Whisk the soy sauce, ginger, garlic, and lime juice together. Marinate the fish 10-15 minutes.
Grill or pan-sear over medium-high heat, 3-4 minutes per side, until it flakes easily.
Top with scallions before serving - mahi mahi is one of the most popular fish on Hawaiian plate lunch menus.

name: Shrimp Scampi (Hawaiian Shrimp Truck Style)
steps: Cook the rice.
Cook the fish in the garlic butter, 6-8 minutes.
Serve over the rice.

ingredients_full.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Enriched Medium Grain Calrose Rice

ingredients_full.note: standing in for whole shell-on shrimp

steps_detailed: Cook the calrose rice in 0.9 cup water, covered, 16-18 minutes.

Melt the butter and cook the garlic gently over low heat, 3-4 minutes, until fragrant but not browned - the garlic butter should be heavy-handed, this dish is defined by how much garlic goes in, not a light hint of it.
Add the fish and cook through, 6-8 minutes, basting with the garlic butter.
Finish with the lemon juice and serve over the rice, spooning the remaining garlic butter over the top.
North Shore shrimp trucks serve this with the shell still on and butter pooling in the plate - the sheer volume of garlic butter is the point of the dish, not a delicate accent.
